data.title: Research Report: Fintech Wins by Taking a Cut, DeFi Generates Trillions in Transaction Volume but Only Earns a Small Portion
data.description:
Source: Artemis & Fintech Architects; Compiled by: BitpushNews

Foreword:

Fintech Architects, in collaboration with the digital finance think tank Artemis, has released its first comparative analysis report on Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) of Fintech and Decentralized Finance (DeFi). If you've ever struggled with whether Robinhood or Uniswap is a more worthwhile investment, then you've come to the right place.

... This report is the first to compare fintech stocks and crypto tokens on the same level. Covering areas such as payments, digital banking, trading, lending, and prediction markets, the report compares revenue, number of users, take rates, industry KPIs, and valuation metrics. The results are astonishing: Hyperliquid's transaction volume has reached over 50% of Robinhood's; the total outstanding loan amount of DeFi protocol Aave has surpassed that of buy-now-pay-later giant Klarna; stablecoin settlement networks are growing much faster than traditional payment providers; and wallets such as Phantom and MetaMask have user bases comparable to digital banking giants like Nubank and Revolut. We found that valuations fully reflect this game: crypto assets are either trading at extremely high premiums or at extremely deep discounts, depending on their expected liquidity. Ultimately, we believe the core issue of their convergence lies in whether the crypto industry learns to build "tollbooths" or the fintech industry adopts the crypto industry's "open track." The Game Between Two Financial Systems For years, we have viewed cryptocurrency and fintech as parallel universes. One is a regulated, audited system traded on Nasdaq; the other is a permissionless system traded on decentralized and centralized exchanges. They share a common language: revenue, volume, payments, lending, trading, but with different "accents." This is changing. With Stripe's acquisition of Bridge, Robinhood's launch of its prediction market, and PayPal minting its own stablecoin, the lines are becoming blurred. The question is, when these two worlds collide, what will the balance of power look like?

Comparison Chart Explanation: In our chart, purple represents cryptocurrencies, and green represents equity companies.

We decided to conduct this experiment: selecting well-known fintech companies in areas such as payments, digital banking, Buy Now Pay Later (BNPL), and retail brokerage, and stacking them against crypto-native counterparts for comparison. We used the same metrics (P/S ratio, ARPU, TPV, number of users, etc.), with green bars representing US stocks and purple bars representing token protocols.

A panoramic view of these two financial systems emerges: on-chain financial protocols often match or even surpass their fintech counterparts in transaction volume and asset size, but they capture only a fraction of the economic benefits. In contrast, crypto asset valuations are either extremely high or extremely low, with very little middle ground. Moreover, their growth rates are completely different.

Payments: The Pipeline for Funds Flows Starting with the largest category in fintech—money transfers. The Green Camp (Giants Abound): PayPal handles $1.76 trillion in transactions annually. Adyen processes $1.5 trillion. Fiserv (the almost forgotten infrastructure layer) processes $320 billion. Block (formerly Square) drives $255 billion through its Cash App and merchant network.

Purple Camp (Artemis' estimated annualized B2B payment volume):

  • Tron moved $68 billion in stablecoins.

  • Ethereum reached $41.2 billion.

  • BNB was $18.6 billion.

  • Solana is valued at approximately $6.5 billion. In absolute terms, the two are not even in the same league. The total stablecoin transaction volume of all major public blockchains is only about 2% of that of traditional fintech payment processors. On the market share chart, the purple bars are almost negligible. But what's interesting is the growth rate: Last year, PayPal's total payment volume grew by only 6%, Block grew by 8%, and European darling Adyen achieved 43% growth (which is very strong by fintech standards). Looking at the blockchains themselves: Tron grew by 493%, Ethereum by 652%, BNB by 648%, while Solana was the fastest, reaching a year-on-year growth rate of 755%. It's important to emphasize that this is an estimate of B2B payment volume based on McKinsey research by the Artemis data team. Conclusion: The growth rate of stablecoins far exceeds that of traditional payment methods, despite their much smaller starting point. Next, let's see who profits the most. Fiserv takes 3.16% of each transaction, Block takes 2.62%, and PayPal takes 1.68%. Even Adyen, which operates on a low-margin business model, takes a 15 basis point cut. As for blockchains, their take rate for stablecoins and asset transfers is extremely low, ranging from approximately 1 to 9 basis points. Blockchains operate by charging gas fees, achieving a significant efficiency advantage over traditional channels by avoiding interchange fees and merchant fees. While this limits the revenue of the underlying protocol, it creates profit margins for payment orchestrators at the upper layers by adding fees. Digital Banks: Wallets Become the New Type of Bank Accounts On the fintech side, we have real licensed banks: Revolut, Nubank, SoFi, Chime, Wise. On the crypto side, we see wallets and yield protocols: MetaMask, Phantom, Ethena, EtherFi. Although they don't have the name "bank," tens of millions of people deposit their assets there and earn interest. User Comparison: Nubank has 93.5 million monthly active users (MAU), making it the world's largest digital bank. Revolut has 70 million users. MetaMask follows closely with 30 million monthly active users, surpassing Wise, SoFi, and Chime in scale. Phantom boasts 16 million monthly active users and has expanded to multiple chains, even launching its own debit card and tokenized shares.

Deposit Scale (Funds Held):

  • Revolut holds $40.8 billion in customer balances.

  • Nubank holds $38.8 billion.

  • SoFi holds $32.9 billion.

  • SoFi holds $32.9 billion.

    • In the crypto space, EtherFi (liquidity restaking) holds $9.9 billion, and Ethena (synthetic dollar) holds $7.9 billion. While this is referred to as TVL (Total Value Locked) within the industry, from a user's perspective, it's simply money stored somewhere to earn yield. Profitability Gap: SoFi's average revenue per user (ARPU) is $264 because it cross-sells across lending, investing, and credit cards. EtherFi boasts an average revenue per user (ARPU) of $256, comparable to SoFi. However, the awkward truth is that EtherFi only has 20,000 active users, while SoFi has 12.6 million. This means that DeFi protocols can efficiently extract value from a niche user base, much like top digital banks, but have yet to reach the masses. In contrast, MetaMask generated approximately $85 million in revenue last year, with an ARPU of only $3.

Valuation Logic:

The market's valuation of the two is surprisingly consistent. Revolut's price-to-sales ratio is 18x, while EtherFi's is 13x and Ethena's is 6.3x. The current trend of convergence is "wallet banking": MetaMask adds debit cards, and Phantom integrates fiat currency channels.

Trading Sector: On-Chain DEXs Challenge Traditional Brokers

In the capital markets, the scale of on-chain exchanges is astonishing.

  • Robinhood processed $4.6 trillion in trading volume over the past 12 months.

  • Hyperliquid (decentralized perpetual contracts) processed approximately $2.6 trillion.

  • Coinbase has processed $1.4 trillion. The aggregated trading volume of major DEXs like Uniswap and Raydium is now on par with Coinbase. This was unimaginable three years ago.

However, the "DEX paradox" lies in the commission rates:

  • Robinhood's overall commission rate is 1.06%, and Coinbase's is 1.03%.

  • The commission rates of the DEX camp are between 3 and 9 basis points.

Rate of commission = LTM revenue / trading volume. eToro, Coinbase, Robinhood, and Bullish's revenue comes from financial statements. Raydium, Aerodrome, Uniswap, and Meteora's revenue comes from Artemis.

This means that Uniswap generates only about $29 million in protocol revenue with $1 trillion in trading volume; while Coinbase generates $14 billion in revenue with $1.4 trillion in trading volume.

This means that Uniswap generates only about $29 million in protocol revenue with $1 trillion in trading volume; while Coinbase generates $14 billion in revenue with $1.4 trillion in trading volume.

Regarding market valuation, the results are consistent with these issues. Coinbase trades at 7.1x sales. Robinhood trades at 21.3x, high for a broker, but supported by growth. Schwab trades at 8.0x, a mature multiple for mature businesses. Uniswap trades at 5.0x fees. Aerodrome trades at 4.8x fees. Raydium trades at 1.3x fees. The market hasn't valued these protocols like it would high-growth tech companies, partly because they generate lower commission rates compared to traditional brokerages. Market capitalization / LTM revenue. Publicly reported market capitalization for the stock is from Yahoo Finance, and the token is from Artemis. The stock price chart shows the direction of sentiment. Since the end of 2024, Robinhood has risen approximately 5.7 times, riding the wave of the retail investment and cryptocurrency recovery. Coinbase has risen 20% over the same period. Uniswap has fallen 40%. Despite a large influx of trading volume into their DEX, the tokens haven't captured much value, partly due to their unclear use as investment vehicles. The only exception is Hyperliquid, which, due to its massive rise, saw gains almost identical to Robinhood's during the same period. While historically DEXs have failed to capture value and be considered public goods, projects like Uniswap are turning on their "fee switch," using fees to burn UNI tokens, now generating $32 million in annualized revenue. We hope that as more trading volume moves on-chain, value will flow back to DEX tokens, with Hyperliquid being a prime example of success. However, until a token holder value capture mechanism like Hyperliquid emerges, DEX tokens will underperform their CEX stock counterparts. Lending: Underwriting the Next Generation Lending is where the contrast becomes more interesting. On one side, you have the core lending product of fintech: unsecured consumer credit. Affirm lets you pay for a Peloton bike in four installments. Klarna does the same for fast fashion. Lending Club pioneered peer-to-peer (P2P) lending before transforming into a true bank. Funding Circle underwrites loans for small and micro businesses. These companies make money by charging borrowers higher fees than they pay depositors, hoping that defaults won't erode the spread. On the other hand, you have collateralized DeFi lending. Aave, Morpho, Euler. Borrowers deposit ETH, lend USDC, and pay an algorithmically determined interest rate. If the collateral drops too much, the protocol automatically sells it. No collection calls or write-offs.
These are businesses with the same name but completely different natures.

Start with the loan ledger. Aave has $22.6 billion in outstanding loans. That's more than Klarna ($10.1 billion), Affirm ($7.2 billion), Funding Circle ($2.8 billion), and Lending Club ($2.6 billion) combined. The largest DeFi lending protocol has a larger loan ledger than the largest BNPL player. Let this fact settle.

Total loan amounts for Lending Club, Funding Circle, Affirm, Klarna, and Figure come from financial reports. Loan deposits for Euler, Morpho, and Aave come from Artemis.

Morpho added another $3.7 billion. Euler, relaunched after its 2023 hack, has $861 million. The DeFi lending stack has grown in total size to rival the entire listed digital lending sector in about four years. But the economic model is inverted.

Funding Circle's Net Interest Margin (NIM) is 9.35% (due to its business model, which resembles private lending). Lending Club's is 6.18%. Affirm, despite being a BNPL company rather than a traditional lender, also achieves 5.25%. These are substantial spreads that compensate for the credit risk these companies absorb through actual execution of underwriting. On the cryptocurrency side, Aave's NIM is only 0.98%. Morpho's is 1.51%. Euler's is 1.30%. Despite having larger loan ledgers, DeFi protocols typically offer lower yields than fintech lenders.

Aave, Euler, Morpho's net interest margin = revenue / loan deposits. Equity net interest margins are derived from financial statements.

DeFi lending is by design overcollateralized. To borrow $100 on Aave, you typically need to deposit $150 or more in collateral. The protocol does not assume credit risk. It assumes liquidation risk; borrowers are paying for leverage and liquidity, not for a privilege of credit access they wouldn't otherwise have access to.

Fintech lenders are doing the opposite. They offer unsecured credit to consumers who want to buy now and pay later. The interest rate spread compensates those who never repay. This is reflected in the actual loss figures from defaults, and managing these losses is a core part of underwriting. Credit loss ratios for stocks come from publicly available financial reports. So which model is better? It depends on what you're optimizing. Fintech lending serves borrowers who need money they don't currently have and assumes real underwriting risk. It's also brutal. Early digital lending institutions (OnDeck, Lending Club, Prosper) have all teetered on the brink of collapse multiple times. Despite its business actually performing well, Affirm's stock price is still down about 60% from its peak, often because underwriting revenue is priced at SaaS multiples without adequately accounting for inevitable later losses. DeFi lending is a leveraged business. It serves those who already own assets but want liquidity and don't want to sell, similar to a margin account. There are no credit decisions here except for the quality of the collateral. It's capital efficient, scalable, and earns a small profit on huge trading volumes. It's also only useful for those who already have a significant amount of assets on-chain and want to earn yield or additional leverage. Prediction Markets: Who Knows? Finally, let's look at prediction markets. These projects represent the latest and most peculiar battleground between Fintech and Decentralized Finance (DeFi). For decades, they were largely academic esoteric, favored by economists but disliked by regulators. The Iowa Electronic Markets once ran small-scale election predictions. Intrade experienced a brief boom before shutting down. Most of these projects were labeled “gambling” or “sports betting.” The idea that you could trade on real-world outcomes and that these markets could produce more accurate predictions than polls or authoritative experts was largely theoretical in the past. This changed in 2024 and accelerated further during the second Trump administration. Polymarket processed over $1 billion in election betting. Kalshi won its lawsuit against the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) and launched political contracts to U.S. users. Robinhood, never missing a trend, also added event contracts. Meanwhile, DraftKings, the giant that already runs the de facto prediction market through Daily Fantasy Sports, quietly made a fortune with a market capitalization of $15.7 billion and revenue of $5.5 billion.

Artemis Forecasting Markets Dashboard

(Chart description: Spot trading volume data for Kalshi and Polymarket comes from Artemis. For DraftKings, trading volume uses "Sportsbook Handle," which is the total amount of settled customer bets in its sports betting products.)

Comparison of revenue for prediction markets (Chart description: Polymarket revenue data comes from Artemis, Kalshi revenue data can be found at the cited link, DraftKings' past 12 months (LTM) revenue data comes from financial reports.)

The huge gap lies in take rates, or in sports betting jargon, "hold".

DraftKings retains 10.57% of every $1 wagered. This is typical of sports betting: the bookmaker takes a cut, provides odds, and manages the risk. Kalshi takes 2.91%, a thinner profit margin more suited to financial exchanges. Crypto-native Polymarket takes only 0.15%. Of its $24.6 billion trading volume, it currently captures a negligible amount of value. This is yet another replay of the dynamics of decentralized exchanges (DEXs). Polymarket's focus is not on value capture, but rather on providing the infrastructure for the existence of prediction markets, matching buyers and sellers, and settling contracts on-chain. It does not employ odds setters, manage balance sheets, or act as your counterparty. While its efficiency is astonishing, monetization is not its core focus. However, investors clearly believe Polymarket can eventually monetize: Polymarket is valued at $9 billion, with a price-to-sales ratio (P/S) of 240. Kalshi is valued at $11 billion, corresponding to $264 million in revenue, with a trading multiple of 42. DraftKings has a trading multiple of only 2.9. Venture capital (VC) simply can't stop pouring money into these platforms, while "traditional" operators like DraftKings and Flutter (FanDuel) watch their stock prices plummet.

Predicted Market Cap Comparison

(Chart description: Kalshi and Polymarket's market caps use the latest private equity valuations. DraftKings' market cap is from Yahoo Finance.)

Polymarket The valuation logic assumes it will either begin monetization in some grand way or evolve into something far larger than the predicted market size. With a price-to-sales ratio exceeding 200, you're not buying a company, but a call option on a completely new financial primitive. Perhaps Polymarket will become the default venue for hedging any real-world event. Perhaps it will add more sports, financial reports, weather, or anything with binary outcomes. Perhaps it will capture a much larger percentage instead of 0.15%, suddenly generating billions of dollars in returns. This is the purest form of the "convergence" problem: will the future belong to regulated exchanges with commission rates and compliance departments, or to permissionless protocols that allow anyone, anywhere, to bet on anything without leaving any profit for the house? The Final Convergence A few years ago, we couldn't compare DeFi and Fintech. But now, the data is there. Cryptocurrencies have built a financial infrastructure that rivals fintech in terms of trading volume, user base, and asset size. Stablecoin tracks are more global than traditional payments, Aave's ledger is larger than Klarna's, and Polymarket is eroding the gaming market share. The technology is working, and products have found their audience. But there's a key "trap": the crypto industry is **far** less adept at capturing economic value (commission rates) than traditional fintech. You can view this as a "feature": it represents the ultimate democratization and efficiency maximization of financial services, benefiting users by destroying profit margins. You can also view it as a "bug": if a protocol cannot generate sufficient revenue, the sustainability of its token value will be challenged. Convergence is happening. Banks are piloting tokenized deposits, the NYSE is researching tokenized stocks, and the total market capitalization of stablecoins has surpassed $300 billion. Fintech giants have seen the future—they won't sit idly by; they will absorb and assimilate it. The question for the next decade is simple: will the crypto world learn to build tollbooths, or will traditional finance learn to utilize the crypto world's path? We bet that both will happen.

data.title: BTC falls out of the top 10 assets; Hang Seng launches Ethereum-tokenized gold ETF
data.description:
Bitcoin

Norway's sovereign wealth fund's indirect Bitcoin exposure grew 149% year-on-year to 9,573 BTC

According to K33, Norway's sovereign wealth fund's indirect Bitcoin exposure grew by 149% in 2025, reaching 9,573 BTC. The fund has made significant investments in companies such as Strategy, MARA, Metaplanet, Coinbase, and Block. While the national fund does not directly purchase cryptocurrencies, many of these companies continue to accumulate Bitcoin. Norway's sovereign wealth fund, managed by Norwegian Bank Investment Management, is one of the world's largest sovereign wealth funds, with assets exceeding $2 trillion, primarily investing in global equities, bonds, and real estate.

Michael Saylor: Kevin Warsh to Become First Fed Chair to Endorse Bitcoin Michael Saylor stated on the X platform that Kevin Warsh will become the first Fed Chair to endorse Bitcoin. Peter Brandt: Bitcoin Will Bottom Out Between August and October and Then Surge Peter Brandt, the renowned trader and chart analyst who successfully predicted the 2018 Bitcoin crash, released a chart analysis this morning pointing out that the ETH price chart has formed a symmetrical triangle pattern, while the total cryptocurrency market capitalization shows an expanding right-angle pattern. Bulls need to prove a breakout to dominate the market, and historically, such patterns often foreshadow further volatility or declines, emphasizing the bears' dominance. Peter Brandt also predicts that Bitcoin will bottom out between August and October and then surge. At least that's how it looks now. BlackRock Deposits $240 Million Worth of Bitcoin and Ethereum into Coinbase According to Onchain Lens, BlackRock deposited 2,288 bitcoins ($188.99 million) and 19,644 ETH ($53.72 million) into Coinbase. Bitcoin's Market Cap Falls Out of Top 10 Global Assets, Currently Ranked 12th Data from 8marketcap shows that Bitcoin's market capitalization has fallen out of the top 10 global assets, currently ranking 12th. Ethereum (ETH) Co-founder: Plans to Launch DAO Security Fund and Deposit Approximately 75,000 ETH to Enhance Ethereum Security Assets associated with The DAO crash in 2016 are being redeployed nearly a decade later to establish a large crypto security fund. That hack directly led to a permanent fork of Ethereum. Griff Green, co-founder of Giveth and one of the original signatories responsible for managing the recovered DAO funds, said Thursday that he will launch the DAO Security Fund, planning to deposit approximately 75,000 ETH (worth about $220 million) to enhance Ethereum's security. This move transforms one of Ethereum's early and most destructive failures into a long-term source of funding for cybersecurity. Unclaimed assets left behind by the DAO crash, which once symbolized the immature stage of the crypto industry, are now being reused to protect an ecosystem that now holds hundreds of billions of dollars in value.

    Unclaimed ETH from The DAO hack 10 years ago will be used to create a $250 million Ethereum security fund

    Unchained podcast host Laura Shin released a video in which Griff Green stated that The DAO is back. Nearly 10 years after The DAO hack in 2016, unclaimed ETH is being used to create an Ethereum security fund worth $250 million. Subsequently, Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin confirmed in a post, "The DAO security fund has officially launched, using more than 75,000 ETH to strengthen Ethereum security." On June 17, 2016, The DAO contract had a vulnerability, and hackers stole approximately 3.6 million ETH. This incident ultimately led to a hard fork of Ethereum, creating the ETH and ETC chains.

    ... Hang Seng Launches Ethereum-Tokenized Gold ETF Hong Kong asset management company Hang Seng Investments has launched a physically backed gold exchange-traded fund (ETF), introducing for the first time a tokenized share class issued on Ethereum, marking the fusion of traditional commodity ETFs and blockchain fund infrastructure. The Hang Seng Gold ETF listed on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange on Thursday under the ticker symbol “03170”, rising approximately 9% in early Asian trading. According to product disclosures, the fund closely tracks the LBMA Gold Price AM and holds physical gold stored in designated vaults in Hong Kong. In addition to the traditional ETF structure, the fund also offers tokenized shares. These tokenized shares are initially issued on Ethereum and may be expanded to other public blockchains in the future. HSBC acts as the tokenization agent for the product. However, although these ETF shares are issued on a public blockchain, they cannot be freely traded on the secondary market; investors can only subscribe or redeem them through qualified distributors. Furthermore, Hang Seng's product page indicates that these tokenized shares are not yet open for subscription and will only be launched after obtaining relevant regulatory approvals. Fidelity will launch the stablecoin FIDD on Ethereum. According to market news, Fidelity will launch the GENIUS-compliant stablecoin FIDD on Ethereum. Vitalik Buterin stated in an article on the X platform that the Ethereum Foundation (EF) will enter a period of "moderate austerity" over the next five years to achieve two main goals: first, to deliver a more aggressive technology roadmap, ensuring that Ethereum continues to be a high-performance, scalable "world computer" without sacrificing robustness, sustainability, and decentralization; and second, to enhance the EF's own long-term sustainability, safeguarding Ethereum's core mission, including the underlying blockchain layer and users' ability to use the network securely, with privacy, and with self-sovereignty. Vitalik Buterin stated that as part of the austerity plan, he will personally take on some of the work that might otherwise have been handled by the foundation's "special projects," focusing on supporting an open, verifiable, end-to-end hardware and software technology stack to protect personal lives and the public environment. This technological vision encompasses areas such as finance, communications, governance, blockchain, operating systems, security hardware, and biotechnology (personal and public health), emphasizing privacy protection, decentralization, and a native-first software architecture. To this end, Vitalik has already withdrawn 16,384 ETH and plans to gradually invest in the aforementioned goals over the next few years, while exploring more secure decentralized staking solutions to use staking rewards for long-term support of related missions. He emphasized that Ethereum itself is an integral part of the vision of "full-stack openness and verifiability." The Ethereum Foundation will continue to focus on Ethereum Core development, but the priority is not "Ethereum everywhere," but rather "Ethereum for people who need it"—serving self-sovereignty, security, and privacy, rather than catering to the needs of centralized enterprises. Vitalik stated that in a world that increasingly values ​​"force to force," this path offers a necessary alternative—building uncontrolled collaborative infrastructure through truly open, verifiable, and user-serving technologies. Bitwise Releases Q4 2025 Enterprise Ethereum Adoption Report: Enterprises Add Over 1 Million ETH Bitwise has released its Q4 2025 Enterprise Ethereum Adoption Report, highlighting the following key developments: 1. Enterprises added over 1 million ETH in Q4 2025. 2. BitMineImmersion's Ethereum holdings surpassed 4 million ETH. 3. BitDigital became the fourth largest Ethereum DAT (Digital Asset Vault). 4. Enterprises hold 5% of the total Ethereum supply. 5. Over 33% of Ethereum Vault enterprises were new entrants in Q4. Other Projects Jiuzi Holdings Receives $90 Million Investment to Advance Blockchain and Web3 Ecosystem Expansion Nasdaq-listed Bitcoin treasury company Jiuzi Holdings announced a strategic cooperation memorandum of understanding with Morgan International Finance Ltd., under which the latter plans to invest $90 million in Jiuzi Holdings through equity investment. Specific investment structures, lock-up periods, and other arrangements will be determined after further negotiation and the signing of legally binding final transaction documents. The new funds will be used to support Jiuzi Holdings' expansion into the blockchain and Web3 ecosystem, as well as the execution of its digital asset strategy. WisdomTree expands its tokenized fund to the Solana blockchain. According to Coindesk, New York-based asset management company WisdomTree has expanded its tokenized fund to the Solana blockchain, driving multi-chain development. Institutional and individual investors can issue, trade, and hold tokenized funds on Solana through WisdomTree Connect and WisdomTree Prime. The Central Bank of the United Arab Emirates (CBA) has approved Universal Digital's issuance of the US dollar stablecoin USDU, in accordance with its Payment Token Services Regulation. The stablecoin is issued and managed by Universal Digital, a crypto company regulated by the Abu Dhabi Global Markets Financial Services Regulatory Authority (ADFLA). USDU is pegged 1:1 to US dollar reserves, with the underlying funds held in secured accounts at onshore banks such as the National Bank of the UAE and Mashreq Bank. This marks the first time a US dollar stablecoin has officially operated under the central bank's payment regulatory framework. Aquanow has been appointed as the global distribution partner, providing access to USDU to institutions outside the UAE in licensed regions. Crypto venture capital firm Escape Velocity raises $62 million to bet on the "DePIN" crypto network, according to Fortune magazine. The funding also covers areas such as telescopes and solar energy. ByteDance and Alibaba to launch new AI model in mid-February, according to The Information.

data.title: The first case of generative "AI illusion" has come to a conclusion: How to define the boundaries of compliance?
data.description:
Introduction

In the past two years, AI assistance has become commonplace in my work.

Taking a recent article I wrote about compliant business operations in the Web3 industry as an example, I needed to search for relevant domestic cases of "unlicensed fund sales constituting illegal business operations," and I tried using DeepSeek for the search.

Its performance can be described as a **perfect misleading**: it not only instantly provided details such as the case number and the court of trial, but also described the defendant's defense logic and the final sentencing in a clear and organized manner, and even attached a **highly misleading** webpage link at the end of the article.

（Image content source: People's Daily Online）

Industry Characterization Debate: "Goods" or "Services"?

Does AI provide "goods" or "services"? This is the most commercially valuable legal characterization in this case, and this question will determine the underlying risk logic of the entire AI industry.

Does AI provide "goods" or "services"? This is the most commercially valuable legal characterization in this case, and this question will determine the underlying risk logic of the entire AI industry. 1. Developers' Core Concern: The Risk of "No-Fault Liability" Large model manufacturers are most wary of being included in the category of "products" under the Product Quality Law. The underlying logic is that "product" liability is subject to the no-fault principle. Simply put, like a pressure cooker exploding, no matter how careful the production process, the manufacturer may still bear liability for compensation. If AI is defined as a "product," then every instance of "hallucination" it outputs could be considered a "product defect." Under current technological conditions, no manufacturer can guarantee the complete elimination of hallucinations, which implies a theoretically unlimited risk of liability. 2. Judicial Determination: AI is a "Service" Rather Than a "Product" The Hangzhou Internet Court astutely pointed out the essential difference between AI and traditional physical goods in its judgment: **unpredictability and interactivity**. The performance of traditional products is determined at the time of manufacture, while the output of AI is random and highly dependent on the algorithm model and user-input prompts. This output is formed jointly by AI and the user, which is more in line with the characteristics of an "intelligence-generated service." 3. Liability Framework: Based on Process, Not Result The court returned the determination of liability for AI services to the **fault-based liability principle** of Article 1165 of the Civil Code, and explained the reasons for this determination. From the logic of the judgment, it can be seen that the law does not require AI output to be absolutely correct, but rather requires service providers to fulfill their duty of care within a reasonable scope. In other words, **AI illusions** themselves do not necessarily constitute a violation of the law. Developers are only held liable when they fail to take reasonable measures to prevent or reduce illusions and are at fault. This definition provides the industry with a clear **technical tolerance range**, the key being how to **determine the substantive and formal standards of a reasonable scope**. **Clarifying the Boundaries:** "Tolerable" Illusions vs. "High-Risk" Illusions Under the principle of "fault-based liability," not all illusions will be attributed to developers. The law's determination of fault is **dynamic**, adjusting in stages based on the stage of technological development and the risks of application scenarios. 1. High Tolerance in Low-Risk Areas In non-professional fields such as creation, entertainment, and general knowledge consultation, users should possess basic discernment abilities. If AI outputs clearly non-common knowledge in casual conversation, and a user suffers harm as a result and seeks legal recourse, their claim may be dismissed due to their failure to exercise reasonable care. In such scenarios, as long as the manufacturer has provided basic risk warnings, courts are generally tolerant of "algorithmic bias." 2. Reasonable Care in Medium-Risk Areas While some entrepreneurial directions have commercial potential, they also carry higher legal risks, requiring extra attention to compliance. For example: In the field of emotional companionship: This field has strong practical application and clear demand, but it may lead to emotional dependence or even improper guidance among users, posing significant ethical and legal risks. In the field of psychological support: If AI provides harmful or misleading advice to users experiencing psychological distress, such as encouraging suicide or incorrect medication, it will directly endanger user safety, and the boundaries of liability will be more stringent. 3. Necessary Caution in High-Risk Areas In the field of professional services, if AI claims to be a "professional lawyer" or "licensed psychological counselor" in its marketing, the court may determine its duty of care based on expert standards. In the event of significant harm, it may incur higher levels of legal liability. Discrepancies between promotional wording and actual capabilities will significantly increase legal risks. 4. Dynamic Duty of Care: What is “Reasonable Effort”? When determining fault, courts typically focus on whether developers have fulfilled the following obligations: Strictly prohibit illegal and harmful content; Clearly indicate the limitations of AI, including clearly informing users of functional limitations, ensuring the prompts are prominent, and providing immediate warnings in high-risk scenarios; Adopt industry-standard technologies to improve reliability, such as using search enhancement generation technologies. Furthermore, commercial factors such as whether the service is charged for and whether third parties are involved and advertising fees are collected may also affect the determination of fault.

Developer Compliance Guide: How to Write a Good "Disclaimer"?

A "disclaimer" is not just a formality, but a key tool for balancing innovation and risk. It clearly conveys the boundaries of services to users and can also prove in judicial review that the operator has fulfilled its necessary notification obligations.

To ensure its effective implementation, it needs to be improved in both form and content: 1. Form: To ensure the effective communication of the disclaimer, three principles must be followed: First, **dynamic reminders** should be proactively displayed when users log in for the first time, when functional modules are updated, or when sensitive scenarios are encountered; Second, **prominent presentation** should be used, with key clauses highlighted in bold and red, and a mandatory reading time can be set; Third, **real-time alerts** should be provided, with the system promptly displaying a warning when users engage in high-risk consultations (such as medical consultations), clearly informing them of the content's reference value and limitations.

2. Substantive Level

Don't try to achieve "one-size-fits-all" exemption from liability; focus on two points:

  • First, clearly define its identity. When AI handles professional issues such as medical and legal matters, it must clearly state its auxiliary nature as a non-professional, for example, by proactively responding, "I am not a professional doctor/lawyer";

  • Second, customize the scenario. For high-risk areas such as medical, psychological, and financial and tax matters, specific notification content and liability agreements should be designed based on the regulatory requirements and risk characteristics of the industry, and a compliance system that matches the depth of its services should be built.

A Brief Discussion Across Industries: When AI Agents Meet Web3

As a lawyer deeply involved in the Web3 field, I believe the Hangzhou court's ruling not only points the way for the compliant operation of AI agents but also provides an important compliance reference for the Web3 industry.

Unlike traditional customer service, some Web3 trading platforms have begun to introduce a Web2-like architecture, integrating AI agents within the software to divert user inquiries. Taking the leading exchange CoinX as an example, users can verify the authenticity of information by @ a specific AI agent. However, to our knowledge, this platform has not yet updated its user agreement or set up a specific disclaimer regarding AI interaction functions, and this lack of compliance may bring significant legal risks.

At the same time, the Web3 field adheres to the principle of "code is law."

If a partially authorized AI agent, due to illusion, exceeds its authority to execute transactions, it will raise a series of complex issues: Does this constitute apparent agency? Are the related legal acts revocable? Can the assets be recovered? Behind these scenarios lie questions such as how to define a clear and explicit scope of authorization, whether to prohibit "fully automated" signatures, and further clarification regarding the self-assumption of risks by AI illusions. In conclusion, this judgment by the Hangzhou court provides valuable institutional buffer space for large-scale model entrepreneurs. It follows the current logic of legal pragmatism, that is, law is a tool serving the direction of mainstream social development. The specific means is to implement rules and provide guidance to the industry through the selection and interpretation of laws. The judge made a good comment in the judgment: AI is an "auxiliary tool," not a "decision substitute." Developers, please accept the buffer provided by this verdict and continue your courageous exploration within the rules; and we, as users, please maintain that precious spirit of skepticism.

data.title: Interest rate cut? Trump's "perfect candidate" might be more difficult to deal with than Powell.
data.description:
Source: The New York Times; Compiled by: BitpushNews

President Trump's announcement of nominating Kevin M. Warsh as the next Federal Reserve Chairman was filled with praise for the 55-year-old former Fed governor with deep ties to Wall Street.

Trump described him as a "central casting" and predicted that Warsh would become "one of the greatest Fed chairs in history, perhaps even the best."

However, Trump also made no secret of how high his expectations were for this most important appointment. Trump wrote on Friday: 'He will never let you down.'

But achieving these expectations will not be easy.

Trump is eager to significantly lower borrowing costs and has done everything in his power to pressure the Fed to cut interest rates.

This pressure even escalated into extreme confrontation—after the Justice Department launched a criminal investigation into Jerome H. Powell regarding whether he lied to Congress about renovations at the Federal Reserve headquarters, the current Fed chairman publicly attacked the administration. Powell, who had long avoided responding to any attacks from Trump, stated that the investigation was merely a pretext to coerce the Fed into lowering interest rates. Later that Friday evening, Trump reiterated his desire for rate cuts, stating that while he hadn't received a clear commitment from Warsh regarding rate cuts, he expected Warsh to do so. "He certainly wants to cut rates; I've been watching him for a long time," Trump said. The path to the ultra-low interest rates Trump demands is fraught with obstacles. Steady economic growth has no need for the approximately 1% interest rate level Trump desires. Central bank officials are well aware of this, as reflected in their near-unanimous decision this week to keep interest rates unchanged at 3.5% to 3.75%. Warsh's own reputation is also a major obstacle to the president's efforts. To be a credible chairman, Warsh's monetary policy decisions cannot be divorced from economic data; otherwise, he risks undermining market confidence and casting doubt on the Fed's commitment to maintaining low and stable inflation. "He'll be trying to play a tightrope: respecting both President Trump's wishes and institutional processes," said Dennis Lockhart, who served as president of the Atlanta Fed from 2007 to 2017, during which time he worked with Warsh. “Believe me, this will be an extremely difficult tap dance. This Fed chair will have to be as graceful and precise as Fred Astaire (the legendary dancer).” Resistance to Rate Cuts If the economy continues to perform as expected over the next year, the resistance to rate cuts, both inside and outside the central bank, could be quite strong. If Warsh is confirmed by the Senate, he won't be able to chair a Fed meeting until June at the earliest, meaning the context could be very different then. However, if economists' predictions are largely accurate, economic growth will accelerate, the labor market will stabilize, and inflation will only gradually ease. In such an environment, there may still be a path to rate cuts, but it will be much more gradual than the president would prefer. To change this, the labor market may need to show significant signs of weakness—far more than most policymakers have predicted. The Federal Reserve's interest rate decisions are voted on by the 12-member Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC). This committee consists of seven governors in Washington, the president of the New York Fed, and four rotating regional Fed presidents. This year, at least three regional presidents on the FOMC are highly skeptical of further rate cuts, including Neel T. Kashkari of the Minneapolis Fed, Lorie K. Logan of the Dallas Fed, and Beth M. Hammack of the Cleveland Fed. While the Fed chair has a dominant voice in interest rate discussions and policy decisions, she only has one vote, meaning Warsh must persuade her colleagues. For decades, Fed chairs have strived to achieve maximum consensus, considered key to clearly communicating policy views and effectively guiding the economy. "You don't want to feel like you're being led by someone else's policies," said James Bullard, who worked with Walsh during his time as president of the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis. Bullard, now dean of Purdue University's business school, added, "If you think a policy is wrong—and everyone takes their responsibilities very seriously—they'll just say, 'No, I don't think this is the right policy.'" Bullard believes that if this happens, "the chairman's job becomes very difficult." Furthermore, if financial markets become concerned about the policies Warsh pursues, it could trigger a market backlash, leading to a rise in long-term interest rates. "To maintain market confidence and credibility, like any Fed chair, Kevin needs to provide a solid analytical foundation for his views based on data and economic models," said Randall S. Kroszner, an economist at the University of Chicago who served alongside Warsh on the Fed Board. "This is also the most powerful way to persuade his colleagues and influence FOMC decisions." He's like a "Swiss Army knife." Those who know Warsh say he will be adept at navigating this challenging environment while also carrying out his calls for "institutional change" at the central bank. In this protracted and highly publicized "interview" for the chairmanship, Warsh, who nearly secured the position during Trump's first term, portrayed himself as someone with a deep understanding of how the Federal Reserve works. He served as a governor for about five years and performed admirably during the global financial crisis. Donald Kohn, who was vice chairman at the time and worked closely with Warsh, commented that Warsh was "invaluable" during that period. "He had a keen eye," Kohn added. "He could tell what the prevailing sentiment was and what he needed to do to guide people to follow him." During his time at the Fed, Warsh consistently expressed concern about nascent inflation and wanted the central bank to be more cautious with its crisis response tools, including its decision to intervene heavily in financial markets and its so-called "quantitative easing" policy of purchasing government bonds. After leaving the Federal Reserve, Warsh maintained this view while working with billionaire investor Stanley Druckenmiller and as a senior fellow at Stanford University's Hoover Institution. While often labeled an "inflation hawk," Warsh has also shown flexibility when economic conditions change. In a 2018 op-ed with Druckenmiller, he called for the Fed to "pause its double whammy of raising rates and tightening liquidity." More recently, Warsh believes there is room for rate cuts because higher growth accompanied by increased productivity—as he argues is happening with the current rise of artificial intelligence—doesn't necessarily lead to higher inflation. He also believes Trump's tariffs are not as inflationary as many fear. Furthermore, he links rate cuts to a broader plan to reduce the Fed's influence in financial markets and shrink its $6.5 trillion balance sheet. Druckenmiller described Warsh as a "Swiss Army knife." He said Warsh was "tested" and possessed the financial market experience required for the job. The Federal Reserve stopped shrinking its balance sheet last year, ending so-called "quantitative tightening" (QT). Druckenmiller stated that pursuing a smaller balance sheet requires meticulous management, which he believes Warsh can handle well. “He’s been in the markets, he’s been at the Fed, he’s not stupid enough to do QT and trigger an economic collapse,” Druckenmiller said. “He absolutely has that market instinct and won’t act at the wrong time.” When asked how Warsh would handle the potential political pressure from the president, Druckenmiller added, “He knows how to deal with people, and I think he’ll handle it as well as possible.” “There probably won’t be any tension because I can’t rule out a scenario of high growth and low inflation. I’m open to any possibility.” Others who have known him for decades believe that Warsh wouldn’t risk his reputation to please the president, a point of praise that has made Powell so highly regarded during his tenure as chairman. "Kevin will only push for rate cuts if he believes a significant cut makes sense," said Michael Boskin, a Hoover Institution scholar and former chairman of the Council of Economic Advisers under President George H.W. Bush. "He will form his own judgment." This assurance is crucial given the intense attacks on the Federal Reserve last year and the deep concerns about its independence from political interference. It also means that when Warsh finally takes office, he will face even more scrutiny, and every decision he makes will be carefully analyzed for potential undue influence. "Regardless of his views on interest rates, I know Kevin understands the importance of the Fed's independence," said Elizabeth A. Duke, a former Fed governor who also worked with Warsh at the central bank. "Hopefully, with his confirmation, Kevin will put an end to the attacks on the Fed's independence."

data.title: A Recap of the Gold Price Crash: In-Depth Analysis of the Impact of New Fed Chair Warsh
data.description:
In-depth review of the gold flash crash and in-depth analysis of the "Wash shock" from the new Fed Chair

This is "Kaiser's Compound Interest Lab". We just experienced a whole month of precious metals' crazy surge, with FOMO permeating the entire market.

However, in the early morning of January 30, 2026, if you were staring at your screen, what you saw wasn't market trends, but a violent reshuffling aimed at clearing out "asymmetric cognition". Gold, like a kite with a broken string above $5580, flash crashed by over 7%, giving back nearly $400, instantly dropping to $5130, and then staging a breathtaking "roller coaster" rebound within a few hours.

Many people's heartbeats probably fluctuated at this frequency.

Today, we'll dissect this "early morning horror" with a scalpel, to see who exactly moved your "grapes" in the code of #EarthOnlineGame.

01 The Fear of the Capuchin Monkey: Why Does a Flash Crash Cause You So Much Pain?

Before analyzing the data, let's talk about our distant relative—the capuchin monkey.

35 million years ago, capuchin monkeys and humans diverged on the path of evolution. These monkeys possess a code called "loss aversion": they would rather have one less grape than accept having one taken away.

Last night, when the gold intraday candlestick chart pulled out a "bearish candlestick" as long as $400, the amygdala in the brains of those staring at the candlestick chart instantly entered "monkey mode."

The fear you feel is more than twice the joy you experience when your assets rise. This is why many people sell at the low of 5,130 and then regret it when it rebounds at 5,450. However, when we look at the candlestick chart over a one-year period, and look carefully to the right, we will notice this small fluctuation.

Finally, when we look at the candlestick chart from a five-year perspective, we hardly notice this fluctuation.

Caesar's famous quote: Investing is a form of self-cultivation. If you examine an asset measured in "ten years" with a "second"-level perspective, you are destined to be killed by the bugs of your brain's evolution.

Prediction coming true?

4.2 Core Risk Points (Black Swan Event) De-escalation of Geopolitical Situation: If the US and Europe reach an unexpected and rapid compromise on the Greenland issue, or if the threat of tariffs is lifted, gold could instantly give back $200-$300 of its geopolitical premium. (This could also cause a liquidity crunch, resulting in a sudden price spike, but it will quickly rebound to a reasonable price). This possibility actually occurred. After a certain trigger was ignited, gold instantly gave back $300-$400. Coupled with profit-taking, and given that the price above $5000 had risen too quickly, there was a liquidity vacuum (a lack of selling pressure during the rise and a lack of buying pressure during the fall), causing a liquidity crunch and further decline to around $5150. Then it quickly rebounded to around $5450. Later, because Trump's likely nominee for the new Federal Reserve chairman was considered "hawkish" (a complex issue, discussed later), all global risk assets (US stocks, cryptocurrencies, etc.) and precious metals fell together. Gold fell back to $5200.

02 In-depth review: Who pressed the "nuclear button" in the early morning?

This flash crash was not without reason, but rather a precise coupling of three forces: "geopolitical situation + monetary policy + liquidity vacuum."

Core trigger: Bessent's "strong dollar" counterattack and the Greenland mystery

Last night, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, in an interview, unusually strongly defended the "strong dollar policy," explicitly denying US involvement in yen intervention. This directly refuted Trump's previous ambiguous attitude of "tacitly approving the depreciation of the dollar."

Meanwhile, an unexpected "cooling signal" emerged in geopolitical tensions—rumors circulated that the US and Europe had reached a preliminary compromise on the Greenland issue, instantly erasing nearly $200 in "war premium." The Fed's "Hawkish Hammer" At the January parliamentary session, the Fed maintained interest rates at 3.5%-3.75%. Powell, in his press conference, not only did not give any expectation of a rate cut, but also displayed extreme "hawkish composure," stating that there was no urgency to cut rates in the short term. For non-interest-bearing gold, this was a bombshell. This is the truth from a technical perspective. Gold prices surged from $4,000 to over $5,000 in the previous week, a steep rise that resulted in insufficient trading volume between $5,100 and $5,500. When long positions took profits at higher levels, selling pressure quickly broke through the weak buy orders, triggering a chain reaction of liquidity panic.

03 Action Guide: What Should I Do?

  • If you are a deep holder (unrealized profit > 15%): Last night's flash crash was just a "unrealized profit pullback" for you. Re-examine: Has the logic behind the collapse of the global order changed? If not, shut down your computer and do something more meaningful in your life.

    If you only entered the market within the last week: You are in a "no margin of safety" zone. Ask yourself: Are you here for short-term trading or to hedge against risk? If the former, theoretically you shouldn't have entered at all; if it's for hedging, accept this "pinpoint" volatility—it's your ticket to the compounding lab. If you don't already hold: The $5150 pinpoint tells you never to chase the steepest upward movement. Wait for liquidity to stabilize and find your "certainty" through phased position building. Since we need to continue studying the fundamentals and long-term logic regardless, let's keep watching.

    04 In-depth Research: The Long-Term Logic and Short-Term Risks of Gold

    Does the long-term logic of gold still hold?

    The conclusion is: It remains rock solid. The core logic of the long-term bull market in gold is not "inflation," but "the entropy increase of sovereign credit."

    • Global de-dollarization: As long as the status of US Treasury bonds as a global safe-haven asset is shaken by sanctions and deficits, the central bank gold-buying spree will not stop.

    • Order restructuring: The more the international order tends towards multipolarity, the higher the value of gold as a "hard currency that transcends sovereignty."

    • Order restructuring: The more the international order tends towards multipolarity, the higher the value of gold as a "hard currency that transcends sovereignty."

The value of major global fiat currencies against gold has approached zero for over a century.

Where are the short-term risks?

The current risk lies in the temporary rebound of "real interest rates".

  • The new Fed Chair's policy inertia: If the policy focus shifts entirely to "curbing inflation" at the expense of liquidity, gold will face an extremely painful period of bottoming out.

  • Price destroys demand: When gold prices approach $6,000, physical jewelry demand in China and India will be physically suppressed, weakening spot support.

05 Power Restructuring: How will the new Fed Chair change the game?

Let's get right to the most professional and hardcore part. The new Fed Chair's inclinations will directly rewrite the asset valuation models of #EarthOnline. When r (discount rate/real interest rate) remains high due to particularly hawkish policies (discussed later), the denominator in the valuation of all assets will increase. Global liquidity contraction: A hawkish bias means the US dollar will remain strong, which will act like a pump, draining liquidity from emerging markets and precious metals. Asset pricing rebalancing: Cryptocurrencies, tech stocks, and precious metals will shift from a broad-based, "flood-like" rise to a brutal "valuation reversion." Gold will exist more as a "defensive cornerstone" than a "speculative target."

06 In-depth Analysis: Bayesian Posterior Model Update (Professional Version)

In the short term, gold is facing the biggest "mechanistic stress test" since the start of this bull market in 2025. data.title: What impact will Warsh's nomination for Federal Reserve Chairman have on BTC?
data.description:
Shan Ouba, Golden Finance

On January 30, 2026, former US President Trump officially announced on the Truth social media platform that he would nominate Kevin Warsh to be the next Chairman of the Federal Reserve. This news quickly attracted widespread attention in global financial markets and the cryptocurrency field. As the core leader of the Federal Reserve, the policy inclinations of the appointee will directly affect the liquidity of the US dollar and global asset pricing. How does Warsh view BTC? What impact will his appointment have on the crypto market?

I. Who is Kevin Warsh?

Kevin Warsh is not an unfamiliar face in the market.

Public information shows that Warsh served as a member of the White House Council of Economic Advisers during the Bush administration and as a member of the Federal Reserve Board of Governors from 2006 to 2011. He was deeply involved in core work such as financial regulatory reform and monetary policy formulation after the global financial crisis, and has a profound understanding of the operating logic of the US financial system.

After leaving the Federal Reserve, Warsh joined the Hoover Institution as a distinguished visiting scholar and also became a partner at the Duquesne family office. During this time, he frequently participated in financial industry seminars and published policy analyses, paying particular attention to the development of emerging financial technology fields. In addition, he has spoken at numerous cryptocurrency industry events, offering clear views on issues such as the nature and regulation of Bitcoin. His stance is neither aggressively bullish nor blindly rejecting, consistently revolving around "compliance, rationality, and marketization." This aligns closely with the Trump administration's approach of promoting the compliant integration of cryptocurrencies into the US financial system, becoming a significant advantage for his nomination as Chairman of the Federal Reserve. II. How Warsh Views Bitcoin 1. For young people, Bitcoin is becoming the new gold. This is Warsh's most frequently quoted and widely disseminated statement. In a 2021 interview on CNBC's *Squawk Box* discussing the monetary environment and asset allocation, Warsh stated: "If you are under 40, bitcoin is your new gold." This statement wasn't about price prediction, but rather a reflection of generational shifts in asset allocation structures. Warsh's core judgment was that in an environment of prolonged monetary easing and sovereign debt inflation, younger generations tend to choose assets that don't rely on government credit and possess a scarcity narrative. Bitcoin, in this respect, is partially assuming the role that gold once played. It's important to emphasize that he didn't claim that "Bitcoin is replacing gold," but rather that it is "diverting" gold's function in investment portfolios. 2. Bitcoin Makes Sense as Portfolio Parts In the same series of interviews, Warsh explicitly stated: In the current macroeconomic and monetary environment, Bitcoin "makes sense as part of a portfolio." He did not describe Bitcoin as a currency, payment instrument, or institutional asset, but rather discussed it within the framework of "asset allocation." This means that, in Warsh's view, BTC is positioned closer to a highly volatile, non-sovereign store of value asset. Its legitimacy stems from diversifying the risks of the traditional financial system, rather than replacing the existing monetary system. This is a typical example of "limited endorsement" from within the central bank system. 3. Bitcoin is both an important asset and a guardian of monetary policy. In a 2025 interview, Warsh stated that Bitcoin is a highly disruptive new technology, an important asset, and even a "health check" for policymakers. "Whether we are right or wrong, its price speaks volumes, so it's more like an inspector of monetary policy." He further added in numerous public speeches that Bitcoin's price fluctuations not only reflect changes in market supply and demand but also reflect changes in market confidence in the Federal Reserve's monetary policy and the dollar system. Its volatility data can serve as a reference for the Federal Reserve in formulating monetary policy. He opposes excessive intervention by the Federal Reserve in the Bitcoin market and advocates respecting market rules and guiding the industry's healthy development through improved regulation. 4. Bitcoin is not an enemy of the financial system. According to a Politico report (end of 2025), Warsh stated that Bitcoin is not an "enemy" of the financial system and should not be excluded from regulation. Instead, it should be incorporated into the traditional financial system through clear regulatory rules to achieve standardized development. He explicitly supports innovative applications of blockchain technology, favoring the classification of Bitcoin as a legitimate asset class. He believes that through regulated stablecoins, spot ETFs, and other compliant pathways, Bitcoin can realize its market value within a controllable scope, while preventing potential risks such as money laundering and speculation. This stance aligns closely with Trump's "America First" crypto agenda. In conclusion, Warsh is neither a Bitcoin advocate nor a simple denier. To date, Warsh has not made any extreme bullish or bearish statements regarding Bitcoin; all his views revolve around "rational viewing, compliant guidance, and realizing its value." With his nomination as Chairman of the Federal Reserve, his regulatory approach to Bitcoin is likely to gradually take shape, influencing the development of the Bitcoin market in the US and globally. His subsequent statements will continue to be a focus of attention in the financial markets and the crypto industry. This means that Bitcoin is more likely to be seen as a real variable that needs to be priced, regulated, and included in a macro-level perspective, rather than something to be simply ignored or administratively rejected.

data.title: Despite Hyper's claim of "strongest liquidity," its true price still depends on Binance.
data.description:
Hyperliquid founder Jeff recently posted that "Hyperliquid has quietly become the most liquid cryptocurrency price discovery platform globally, with order book depth for BTC perpetual contracts surpassing Binance." At first glance, this seems like a groundbreaking "milestone"—an on-chain derivatives platform surpassing Binance to become the global price discovery center.

However, a basic understanding of the trading market reveals that comparisons like Hyperliquid's are essentially typical "order book performances," not equivalent to real liquidity, much less price discovery power. In fact, this claim could be a misinterpretation of market mechanisms, or even marketing hype.

(Screenshot posted by Jeff: Binance on the left / Hyper on the right) A large number of orders does not equal strong liquidity; Hyper's "depth" is a mechanism illusion. The screenshot shown by Jeff is a typical Orderbook depth comparison. The problem is that Hyperliquid's matching mechanism is not a real-time, high-frequency matching mechanism. A widely discussed fact in the industry is that Hyperliquid matching has a significant latency window (even jokingly referred to by the community as "matching only once every 80 seconds"). This means that user orders are not executed immediately, and a large number of orders accumulate in the order book. The order book looks "thick," but the actual execution efficiency is very low. It's like a farmers' market: Binance is a "real-time exchange," where buy and sell orders are executed instantly. Hyper is more like a "timed auction," where orders pile up waiting for settlement. The result is that Hyper's order depth is more like "stacking depth" than "execution depth." True liquidity must answer one question: Can you execute large trades quickly with extremely low slippage? Binance can, but Hyper may not. Price discovery isn't about order books, it's about "who the global market follows." Jeff says Hyper has become a "global price discovery center." But what are the standards for price discovery? This isn't a screenshot, but rather who determines market prices. Who is the primary reactionary force for global funds? Who has the largest trading volume and open interest? Whose index is cited across the entire industry? The answer is clear: Binance remains the largest pricing anchor for BTC perpetual futures globally. In the real market, CME influences institutional funds, Binance influences global retail investors and market makers, OKX, Bybit, and others follow suit, and the on-chain Perp platform passively follows. Most mainstream market indices (CoinMarketCap, TradingView, Coinalyze) assume that BinancePerp is the core source of BTC price discovery. Even with Hyperliquid's trading volume growth, it falls far short of the level of a "global pricing center." | Trading volume, user base, market maker ecosystem: Hyper cannot compare to Binance. Binance's advantage is not "order screenshots," but its complete financial ecosystem. Binance boasts the world's largest derivatives trading volume; for a long time, Binance's perpetual contract daily trading volume has consistently reached tens to hundreds of billions of dollars. Liquidity remains even during periods of extreme market volatility. Hyperance's size is still in the "new platform growth phase" and cannot support global price discovery. Binance boasts the world's most mature market maker system. Binance's liquidity comes from top-tier market maker institutions such as Jump, Wintertermute, GSR, and Cumberland. These institutions deploy real funds and risk management models on Binance. Hyper's so-called liquidity, however, largely stems from incentive-driven mechanisms, LP rewards, and performative order placement. Liquidity isn't built on screenshots; it's built on long-term capital investment. | The deepest liquidity outside of traditional financial exchanges? More of a gimmick than a fact. Jeff also mentioned that Hyper has become the deepest market for traditional asset perpetual contracts. It sounds impressive, but the question is: where is the real pricing center for TradFi asset derivatives? The S&P 500 is on CME, gold on COMEX, forex on EBS, and US Treasury bonds on the Broker system. Creating an "Apple stock contract" on-chain perpetual contract won't change the TradFi pricing system. Hyper's so-called TradFiPerp (traditional assets) is more of a synthetic asset game within the crypto world. It's a long way from being a houseall of finance (asset center). Binance's advantage lies in real trading, a stable system, and global trust. Hyper can market itself as "upgrading the financial system," but Binance is already doing it, boasting the world's largest user base, strongest risk control system, most mature matching engine, most complete product matrix, and broadest regulatory integration path. Binance doesn't win by order book thickness, but by real trading experience, millisecond-level matching, ability to execute trades even in extreme market conditions, the world's largest liquidity pool, and the lowest slippage—real market depth. Hyper's order book might look more "impressive," but Binance's execution is the "real" result. Hyperliquid's "liquidity myth" is more like marketing hype. Hyperliquid's growth is noteworthy, but Jeff's statement is clearly an exaggeration. The market won't change its pricing center based on a screenshot of an order book. Price discovery belongs to the largest trading volume, the largest liquidity pool, the most mature market-making system, the fastest matching engine, and the broadest global user base. In these metrics, Binance remains ahead. Hyper could argue that it has "thicker order books," but Binance, which Jeff is comparing itself to, is the true price heart of the global crypto market.

data.title: Kevin Warsh, the next chairman of the Federal Reserve
data.description:
According to US media reports, on January 30th, US President Trump met with former Federal Reserve Governor Kevin Warsh at the White House and, in a subsequent phone call, formally asked him if he would be willing to serve as the next Federal Reserve Chairman. Warsh ultimately gave an affirmative answer. This development was quickly reflected in the prediction market. On Polymarket, the contract for "Who will Trump nominate as Fed Chair" is almost unanimously in favor: Kevin Walsh's "Yes" has a probability as high as 94%. Rick Reid has only 4% remaining, with a total transaction volume of $310 million. The market has almost accepted this outcome as a foregone conclusion. Although current Chairman Powell's term doesn't end until May 15th, this early succession arrangement clearly carries a distinct political signal. Why now? On the surface, this appears to be a normal preparation for the transfer of power. But the differences between Trump and himself have long been public: persistently high interest rates, a tight monetary policy pace, and an awkward coordination with fiscal stimulus. In Trump's view, the Fed's willingness to align with the overall economic narrative is more important than simply cutting interest rates. Pre-selecting a successor not only paves the way for the future but also exerts silent pressure on the current administration. Who is Warsh?

Kevin·WarshKevin Warsh),1970born in 1970 into a middle-class family in Albany, New York, and grew up in the suburbs. He repeatedly mentioned that his understanding of the real economy came from places far removed from Wall Street, places devoid of financial jargon, where only business, employment, and cash flow existed. His educational path was extremely elite. He received his undergraduate degree in Public Policy from Stanford University, majoring in Economics and Politics; and his JD from Harvard Law School. After graduating, he didn't pursue a legal career but went directly to Wall Street. From 1995 to 2002, he worked in the mergers and acquisitions department of Morgan Stanley, rising from analyst to executive director, deeply involved in large-scale transactions and developing an extremely keen sense for market signals. In 2002, he joined the Bush administration as Special Assistant to the President for Economic Policy, responsible for capital markets, banking, and insurance regulation. Four years later, at the age of 35, Warsh was nominated to the Federal Reserve Board of Governors, becoming one of the youngest governors in the history of the Federal Reserve at the time. During his tenure from 2006 to 2011, which spanned the global financial crisis, he was both a policymaker and a "firefighter." He played a crucial role in transforming Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs into bank holding companies to secure emergency funding from the Federal Reserve, and was often seen as a bridge between the Federal Reserve and Wall Street. During the 2008 financial crisis, he would call Jamie Dimon (CEO of JPMorgan Chase) or Lloyd Blankfein (CEO of Goldman Sachs) to understand the actual market positions and risks. In 2011, he strongly opposed Bernanke's launch of a second round of quantitative easing (QE2), arguing that this "mad printing of money" was mortgaging the future and would exacerbate the wealth gap. When he found he could not persuade others on the committee, he resigned. After leaving office, Warsh entered the academic and private spheres, including serving as a scholar at the Stanford Hoover Institution and a lecturer at business schools, while also joining the boards of companies such as UPS and Coupang. Warsh consistently emphasized rule-based operations, realistic strategies, and a decision-making approach focused on maintaining the Federal Reserve's credibility at crucial moments. His personal background also supported his political and financial networks. In 2002, he married Jane Lauder, an heiress to the Estée Lauder family fortune. This marriage brought him into the core circles of American capital and politics, and established a close relationship with Trump. With his keen market analysis and communication skills, he thrived in elite social circles. He possessed both the acumen of a financier and the eloquence of a "financial diplomat," earning not only the respect of the establishment but also, years later, the ability to gain a following among the unpredictable Trump. In recent years, Warsh's stance has undergone subtle adjustments. While not repudiating his past positions, he has begun to publicly state that failing to cut interest rates at crucial moments could damage the Federal Reserve's credibility and impact economic stability. The market generally interprets this as a realistic adjustment; he is no longer merely a dogmatic hawk, but a pragmatist who emphasizes rules while understanding how to adjust his stance in the face of political realities.

Why did Trump choose him?

Wash's victory in the final shortlist was quite dramatic.

... Just a few days ago, BlackRock executive Rick Rieder was considered the biggest dark horse. While both Rick Rieder and Kevin Hassett have their strengths, for Trump, the logic behind his selection is that trust outweighs political stance. Walsh pinpointed Trump's three core demands during the interview. Warsh explicitly stated that the Federal Reserve "must cut interest rates" and suggested making room for interest rate adjustments by shrinking the Fed's balance sheet. This proposition perfectly aligns with Trump's economic vision of "low interest rates, high growth." At the same time, Warsh has long been a public critic of Powell, accusing the Federal Reserve of systemic errors in its inflation management and emphasizing that his position is consistent with Trump's economic philosophy. What's even more noteworthy is that Walsh excels at using language easily understood by Trump to depict the future, predicting that AI will bring about a "Reagan-era productivity boom," which aligns perfectly with Trump's vision of "making Trump great again." Ultimately, Trump chose "one of his own people he trusted more," rather than simply focusing on candidates with policy leanings. The market's timely reaction to the news of Warsh's selection quickly triggered a chain reaction in the financial markets. Bitcoin plunged more than 5% in the short term, once falling to $85,200. Gold spot prices plummeted by 3.2%, hitting a low of around $5,300 per ounce, while silver prices fell by more than 8%. The US Dollar Index (DXY) rose 0.38% in the short term, briefly touching 104.82, a new high in nearly a week. US stock futures fell across the board, with Nasdaq 100 futures down 0.72%, S&P 500 futures down 0.51%, and Dow Jones futures down 0.42%. U.S. Treasury yields generally rose, with the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield rising by about 7 basis points to around 4.28%; the 2-year yield rose by 5 basis points to... lang="EN-US">3.92%. The market's expectation for the total number of interest rate cuts in 2026 has also been adjusted, from approximately 3.5 times (100 basis points) to 2.3-2.5 times (60-65 basis points). The probability of a 25bp rate cut in three months has decreased from 75% to 58%. This is not simply a game of interest rates, but a contest between financial policy rules and market expectations. Despite Warsh's support for interest rate cuts, which is bearish for the dollar in the short term, the market is also focused on his hawkish stance, tightening of liquidity, and opposition to "money printing." This expectation directly outweighs the short-term benefits of interest rate cuts, putting pressure on Nasdaq futures and other risk assets. In the medium term, Warsh emphasizes "rule-based" operations and opposes excessive intervention. This could provide the market with a more predictable policy framework, but it also means stricter regulation and operations. Bond yields face upward pressure, dollar assets benefit, while equities and emerging market assets face certain challenges. In the long term, the narrative of the Fed's independence will be tested. Although operations may return to rule-based and normalized practices, the White House's increasing influence may cause market confidence in the Fed's policies to fluctuate. Warsh may also cooperate with Trump's fiscal expansion policies within the framework of monetary policy, such as tax cuts, to offset fiscal expansion with tight monetary policy in order to avoid a repeat of high inflation.

Crossroads in the Crypto Market

Wash's attitude towards crypto is somewhat complex; he is neither completely opposed nor fervently supportive.

Wash's attitude towards crypto is somewhat complex; he is neither completely opposed nor fervently supportive.
